request.question: Who is able to fill a larger variety of roles in the entertainment industry, Dave Moody or Ransom Riggs?

title: Ransom Riggs
Library of Souls
No Limit Kids: Much Ado About Middle School
Dave Moody
Hollow City (novel)
Season of Miracles
Miss Peregrine's Home for Peculiar Children
Entertainment industry in India
The Moody Brothers
Praise Band: The Movie
sentences: Ransom Riggs (born February 3, 1979) is an American writer and filmmaker best known for the book "Miss Peregrine's Home for Peculiar Children".
Library of Souls is a sequel to 2014 novel "Hollow City" written by Ransom Riggs and third book in the series of "Miss Peregrine's Home for Peculiar Children".
It was released on September 22, 2015 by Quirk Books.
No Limit Kids: Much Ado About Middle School is a 2010 direct-to-video family comedy film.
It starred Bill Cobbs, Lee Meriwether, Blake Michael, Celeste Kellogg and Ashton Harrell.
The screenplay was written by Joshua Moody and the film was directed by Dave Moody for Elevating Entertainment Motion Pictures, who also provided the musical score for the film.
David B. Moody is an artist, producer, songwriter and filmmaker from North Carolina.
His instrumental proficiency has earned him two Grammy Award Nominations and three International CMA Awards as a member of The Moody Brothers.

After becoming regular performers on the Grand Ole Opry and touring extensively throughout Europe in the 80s, the trio of Carolina brothers signed a contract with the Walt Disney Company in 1992 to open and perform their own nightly concerts at Disneyland Paris' Disney Village, where they performed for over 50 million guests during their time in France.
Hollow City is a 2014 dark fantasy novel and a sequel to "Miss Peregrine's Home for Peculiar Children" written by Ransom Riggs.
It was released on January 14, 2014 by Quirk Books.
The novel is set right after the first, and sees Jacob and his friends fleeing from Miss Peregrine's to the "peculiar capital of the world", London.
Season of Miracles is a 2013 American Christian-themed dramatic film directed by Dave Moody and starring John Schneider, Grayson Russell, Andrew Williams, and Nancy Stafford.

Based on writer Rusty Whitener's award winning novel "A Season of Miracles", the story is set in Alabama during the 1970s and follows a group of twelve-year-olds and their championship little league baseball season.
Miss Peregrine's Home for Peculiar Children is a contemporary fantasy debut novel by American author Ransom Riggs.
The story is told through a combination of narrative and vernacular photographs from the personal archives of collectors listed by the author.
The media and entertainment industry in India consists of many different segments under its folds such as television, print, and films.
It also includes smaller segments like radio, music, OOH, animation, gaming and visual effects (VFX) and Internet advertising.
Entertainment industry in India has registered an explosive growth in last two decades making it one of the fastest growing industries in India.
From a single state owned channel, Doordarshan in the 1990s there are more than 400 active channels in the country.
Worldwide, 2010 saw the global economy begin to recover from a steep decline in 2009.
Improved economic conditions in 2010 played a major role in a rebound in customer spend.
Since the world economy begin to recover from the global financial crisis of 2008, improved economic conditions played a major role in rebound in consumer spend.
While India was not critically impacted by the downturn in 2008 and 2009, it demonstrated one of the highest growth rates this year and continued to at a healthy pace.
The rising rate of investments by the private sector and foreign media and entertainment (M&E) majors have improved India's entertainment infrastructure to a great extent.
As per the recent report by PricewaterhouseCoopers (PwC), Indians are likely to spend more on entertainment in the coming years with a steady growth in their disposable income.
And as per the combined survey report by KMPG and FICCI, the entertainment industry in India is expected to expand by 12.5% every year and is likely to reach US$20.09 billion by the year 2013.
The industry pegged at INR 5808 billion in 2009 as compared to INR 3565 billion in 2005.

The Indian Media & Entertainment Industry grew from US$12.9 billion in 2009 to US$14.4 billion in 2010, a growth of 11 per cent, according to a report by the Federation of Indian Chambers of Commerce and Industry (FICCI) and research firm KPMG.
The report also states that backed by positive industry sentiment and growing media consumption, the industry is estimated to achieve growth of 13 per cent in 2011 to touch US$16.2 billion.
As the industry braces for exciting times ahead, the sector is projected to grow at a CAGR of 14 percent to reach US$28.1 billion by 2015.

The Moody Brothers are an Americana country music trio who gained prominence in 1985 when they were nominated for a Grammy Award for their instrumental performance of the classic fiddle tune "Cotton Eyed Joe".
This trio of brothers, Carlton Moody, Dave Moody, and Trent Moody were nominated for a second Grammy in 1990 and won three International Country Music Awards in Europe.
Praise Band: The Movie is a 2008 faith-based film by Elevating Entertainment Motion Pictures.
It starred George Hamilton IV, Adam Melton and James Dana Bryan.
The screenplay was written by Joshua Moody and the film was produced and directed by Dave Moody.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

hop1_docs: Ransom Riggs | Ransom Riggs (born February 3, 1979) is an American writer and filmmaker best known for the book "Miss Peregrine's Home for Peculiar Children".

No Limit Kids: Much Ado About Middle School | No Limit Kids: Much Ado About Middle School is a 2010 direct-to-video family comedy film.  It starred Bill Cobbs, Lee Meriwether, Blake Michael, Celeste Kellogg and Ashton Harrell.  The screenplay was written by Joshua Moody and the film was directed by Dave Moody for Elevating Entertainment Motion Pictures, who also provided the musical score for the film.

Praise Band: The Movie | Praise Band: The Movie is a 2008 faith-based film by Elevating Entertainment Motion Pictures.  It starred George Hamilton IV, Adam Melton and James Dana Bryan.  The screenplay was written by Joshua Moody and the film was produced and directed by Dave Moody.

The Moody Brothers | The Moody Brothers are an Americana country music trio who gained prominence in 1985 when they were nominated for a Grammy Award for their instrumental performance of the classic fiddle tune "Cotton Eyed Joe".  This trio of brothers, Carlton Moody, Dave Moody, and Trent Moody were nominated for a second Grammy in 1990 and won three International Country Music Awards in Europe.

Dave Moody (sportscaster) | David Wayne "Dave" Moody is an American motorsports commentator who works for the Motor Racing Network.  He is the lead turn announcer for MRN and has worked for the radio network on and off since 1983.  He moved to MRN full-time in 1997 and became the lead turn announcer in 2001.  Since 2003, Moody has been the host of the nationally syndicated Sirius XM show "Sirius XM Speedway" on Sirius XM NASCAR Radio.
Dave Nutting | David Judd Nutting is an industrial design engineer who played a role in the early video game industry.  He is a graduate of the Pratt Institute with a degree in industrial design.

Ransomes Industrial Estate | Ransomes Industrial Estate, also known as Ransomes Europark is combined retail and business park on the South-Eastern edge of Ipswich in Suffolk, UK.  It takes its name from Ransomes, Sims & Jefferies, which still maintains a presence on the park.

response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
1) Evidence found:
- Ransom Riggs: passage [1] — "Ransom Riggs (born February 3, 1979) is an American writer and filmmaker" (quote tokens: "American writer and filmmaker"; "born February 3, 1979") — see passage [1].
- Dave Moody (entertainment/music): passage [2] — "the film was directed by Dave Moody" and "also provided the musical score" (quote tokens: "directed by Dave Moody"; "provided the musical score") — see passage [2]. Passage [3] — "the film was produced and directed by Dave Moody" (quote token: "produced and directed by Dave Moody") — see passage [3]. Passage [4] — The Moody Brothers include "Carlton Moody, Dave Moody, and Trent Moody" and are an "Americana country music trio" (quote tokens: "Dave Moody" and "Americana country music trio") — see passage [4].
- Alternate Dave Moody: passage [5] — "Dave Moody (sportscaster) ... American motorsports commentator" (quote token: "Dave Moody (sportscaster)") — see passage [5].

2) Ambiguities:
- Multiple candidate "Dave Moody" pages exist in the passages: the entertainment/music Dave Moody (credited as director/producer/musical score contributor and member of The Moody Brothers) versus "Dave Moody (sportscaster)". The passages do not present a single canonical "Dave Moody" occupations page.
- No disambiguation/occupation list for either person is present in the passages; Ransom Riggs has a clear lede token, but Dave Moody's complete occupation list is scattered across film and music passages.

3) Bridge entity / Missing fact:
- Bridge entities to fetch: "Ransom Riggs" and "Dave Moody" (the entertainment/music figure as referenced in passages). Precise missing fact needed: the full, authoritative list of occupations/roles for each person (e.g., the lede/infobox "Occupation(s)" or Career/Filmography/Discography credits) so we can count/compare how many distinct entertainment-industry roles each fills.

4) Retrieval direction (prioritized next hops):
- Open "Ransom Riggs" page — check lede/infobox and Career/Works/Filmography sections for listed occupations (search: "Ransom Riggs occupations lede infobox writer filmmaker").
- Open "Dave Moody" page(s):
  a) Fetch the Dave Moody page associated with Elevating Entertainment / The Moody Brothers (check lede/infobox, Career, Filmography, Discography, and "The Moody Brothers" page) (search: "Dave Moody musician director producer 'The Moody Brothers' lede").
  b) Fetch "Dave Moody (sportscaster)" page to confirm this is a separate person and not the entertainment figure (check lede/infobox).
- Also open the film pages already cited ("No Limit Kids: Much Ado About Middle School" and "Praise Band: The Movie") to verify credits sections if needed (check "Credits" or lede).

Do not produce the final comparison yet — the final answer will be given after retrieving the above pages and extracting each person's complete list of occupations/roles.

hop2_docs: Ransom Riggs | Ransom Riggs (born February 3, 1979) is an American writer and filmmaker best known for the book "Miss Peregrine's Home for Peculiar Children".
Marquess of Lede | The Marquess of Lede (Spanish: Marquesado de Lede) was a Flemish title in use during the Ancien Regime, Lede is a city in Flanders.

Dave Moody (sportscaster) | David Wayne "Dave" Moody is an American motorsports commentator who works for the Motor Racing Network.  He is the lead turn announcer for MRN and has worked for the radio network on and off since 1983.  He moved to MRN full-time in 1997 and became the lead turn announcer in 2001.  Since 2003, Moody has been the host of the nationally syndicated Sirius XM show "Sirius XM Speedway" on Sirius XM NASCAR Radio.

The Moody Brothers | The Moody Brothers are an Americana country music trio who gained prominence in 1985 when they were nominated for a Grammy Award for their instrumental performance of the classic fiddle tune "Cotton Eyed Joe".  This trio of brothers, Carlton Moody, Dave Moody, and Trent Moody were nominated for a second Grammy in 1990 and won three International Country Music Awards in Europe.

Library of Souls | Library of Souls is a sequel to 2014 novel "Hollow City" written by Ransom Riggs and third book in the series of "Miss Peregrine's Home for Peculiar Children".  It was released on September 22, 2015 by Quirk Books.

Älvsborg Ransom (1613) | Älvsborg ransom was an indemnity, stipulated in the Treaty of Knäred 1613, that would redeem Älvsborg Castle (in Gothenburg, Sweden) from Danish military occupation of the Kalmar War.  According to the treaty, the ransom was one million rixdollars.  The ransom would be financed by a nationwide tax, which would be paid during each of the six years 1613-1618 by the entire population of Sweden.  The Danes did not only hold Älvsborg Castle as a collateral for the ransom, but also the towns of Nya Lödöse, Old Lödöse and Gothenburg, as well as seven hundreds of Västergötland.  Yet, since the return of Älvsborg was uppermost in the mind of the government, the ransom and the tax to pay for it has in history been named after this castle.

Lede Formation | The Lede Formation (Dutch: "Formatie van Lede" ; abbreviation: Ld) is a geologic formation in the subsurface of Belgium.  The formation is named after the town of Lede in East Flanders.  It consists of shallow-marine limestone and sandstone, deposited in the former sea that covered Belgium during the Eocene.
